1.lecture:
§1 · The diode and rectification

A diode conducts current in one direction only — forward-biased, once forward voltage exceeds roughly \(0.6\)–\(0.7\ \text{V}\) for silicon, it presents low resistance; reverse-biased, it blocks, up to its rated breakdown voltage. A single diode gives half-wave rectification, passing only one half of the AC cycle. Four diodes in a bridge configuration give full-wave rectification, redirecting both halves of the cycle the same way — using the source more completely and, critically, doubling the ripple frequency relative to a half-wave circuit (twice the mains frequency instead of once), which matters directly for the next section.

§2 · Filtering and ripple

A capacitor placed across the rectified output smooths the pulsing waveform: it charges to the peak, then discharges slowly through the load between peaks, and is refilled at the next one. The residual variation left riding on the DC output is ripple. Larger capacitance, relative to load current, means a longer discharge time constant between refills and therefore less droop — smaller ripple. Full-wave rectification helps further, since the capacitor is refilled twice as often as with half-wave, for the identical component values.

§3 · Regulation and the datasheet numbers

A voltage regulator — linear or switching — takes the filtered-but-still-rippling DC and holds the output constant despite both input variation and load-current changes. Regulation specifically means how well output voltage stays constant as something else varies: line regulation is the response to input voltage changes, load regulation is the response to output current changes — genuinely different specifications from ripple, which describes the raw filtered waveform before regulation cleans it up further. A rectifier-plus-regulator chain runs: AC in, rectified (pulsing DC), filtered (DC plus ripple), regulated (clean, stable DC) — reading a power-supply datasheet means recognizing which stage each spec actually describes.

§4 · The 24 VDC world

24 VDC is the near-universal control-panel and field-instrumentation standard because it threads a genuine engineering needle: high enough above typical wiring voltage-drop and noise margins to travel realistic field distances without the signal degrading, yet low enough to fall under most jurisdictions' extra-low-voltage safety threshold, commonly \(50\ \text{VDC}\) — meaning 24 VDC field wiring and terminals can be worked on with far lighter safety precautions than mains-voltage wiring. Nearly every PLC I/O card, sensor, and field transmitter in an industrial plant runs on or interfaces at 24 VDC for exactly this reason — a safety-driven engineering standard, not a historical accident.

Key chain — a power supply's stages

AC in → rectified (pulsing) → filtered (DC + ripple) → regulated (clean, stable DC). Each stage has its own, different spec.

quiz.q:
Reading two different datasheet specs

Problem. A power supply datasheet lists "ripple: 50 mV p-p" and "load regulation: ±0.1 V over 0–2 A." Explain what each spec actually describes, and why they are different things.
quiz.solution: Solution. Ripple describes the residual AC variation left on the DC output after filtering — a raw waveform-quality spec, independent of load changes. Load regulation describes how much the already-regulated output shifts as load current changes from 0 to 2 A — a control-response spec. A supply can have very low ripple but poor load regulation, or the reverse; they measure different failure modes.

quiz.q:
Half-wave vs. full-wave ripple

Problem. A half-wave rectified 50 Hz supply and a full-wave (bridge) rectified 50 Hz supply are filtered with identical capacitors and identical load current. Which has lower ripple, and why?
quiz.solution: Solution. Full-wave has lower ripple. It refills the filter capacitor twice per mains cycle (100 Hz ripple frequency) instead of once (50 Hz), giving the capacitor less time to discharge between refills — less droop, for identical capacitance and load.

quiz.q:
Why 24 VDC, specifically

Problem. Explain, using both a safety threshold and a signal-distance argument, why 24 VDC — rather than 5 VDC or 120 VDC — became the industrial field-wiring standard.
quiz.solution: Solution. 24 VDC sits below the common extra-low-voltage safety threshold (often 50 VDC), so field wiring and terminals can be worked on with far lighter precautions than mains-voltage wiring — a real safety benefit. It is also high enough above typical noise levels and wiring voltage-drop to travel realistic field distances without degrading the way a lower voltage like 5 V would over the same wire runs and connector resistances.

quiz.q: A diode conducts current:
choices: In one direction only, when forward-biased
In both directions equally
Only when reverse-biased
Only at exactly 0 V
quiz.solution: Forward bias overcomes the diode's forward voltage drop and allows conduction.

quiz.q: A bridge rectifier (4 diodes) compared to a single diode:
choices: Gives full-wave rectification, using both halves of the AC cycle
Blocks all current entirely
Only works on DC input
Gives half-wave rectification
quiz.solution: Both half-cycles are redirected the same direction through the load.

quiz.q: Ripple describes:
choices: The residual AC variation on a filtered DC output
The output's response to load current changes
The diode's forward voltage drop
The regulator's efficiency
quiz.solution: Ripple is a property of the filtered waveform itself.

quiz.q: Larger filter capacitance, for the same load current:
choices: Reduces ripple voltage
Increases ripple voltage
Has no effect on ripple
Only affects regulation, never ripple
quiz.solution: A larger cap discharges more slowly between refills.

quiz.q: Voltage regulation refers to:
choices: How well output voltage stays constant despite input or load changes
The raw ripple on the rectified waveform
The diode's breakdown voltage
The mains supply frequency
quiz.solution: Regulation is specifically about stability under changing conditions.

quiz.q: Line regulation specifically measures:
choices: Output voltage response to input voltage changes
Output voltage response to load current changes
Ripple frequency
Diode forward voltage
quiz.solution: Load regulation is the load-current counterpart to this spec.

quiz.q: 24 VDC is the industrial standard largely because:
choices: It sits below common extra-low-voltage safety thresholds while still practical for field-wiring distances
It is the highest voltage available from a wall outlet
It requires no regulation at all
It was chosen arbitrarily with no engineering basis
quiz.solution: A genuine safety-and-practicality compromise, not a historical accident.

quiz.q: In a rectifier-plus-regulator chain, the order of stages is:
choices: Rectify, then filter, then regulate
Regulate, then rectify, then filter
Filter, then regulate, then rectify
All three happen simultaneously with no order
quiz.solution: Each stage builds on the output of the one before it.

1.kuwait:
A control panel's 24 VDC power supply running noisy control logic (an intermittently misbehaving relay, a PLC input that occasionally reads wrong) is a routine troubleshooting entry point at §2 and §3: an aging filter capacitor with rising ripple, or a supply whose load regulation has drifted as it has aged, can produce symptoms that look like a wiring or logic fault until someone actually scopes the 24 V rail and checks it against the nameplate ripple and regulation specs.

Field technicians commissioning a new instrument loop treat the 24 VDC safety threshold from §4 as a real, practical boundary — it is why loop-powered field devices can often be wired and tested live with standard precautions, while the same technician treats the panel's incoming mains supply with an entirely different, more formal lockout procedure, even though both circuits sit in the same cabinet.

2.lecture:
§1 · The transistor as a switch

Driven by a small base (or gate) current or voltage, a transistor can control a much larger current through its collector (or drain) — the basis of both amplification and switching. Used as a switch, the goal is to avoid the linear amplifying region entirely and drive the device hard into one of two extremes: cutoff, where base drive is insufficient and essentially no collector current flows — the switch is open; or saturation, where base drive is large enough that the transistor is fully on, collector current is set by the external circuit, and the voltage drop across the transistor itself is small — the switch is closed.

§2 · Why lingering in between matters — heat

Power dissipated in the transistor is (approximately) collector current times the voltage across it. In cutoff, current is near zero — negligible power, regardless of voltage. In saturation, voltage is near zero — negligible power, regardless of current. In the linear region between them, both current and voltage can be substantial simultaneously, and their product — real dissipated power — can be large. A switching circuit is designed specifically to cross that middle region quickly, not to idle there, which is why switching transistor circuits stay cool while linear amplifier stages, deliberately biased into that middle region, need real heat-sinking.

§3 · Driving relays and indicator loads

A transistor switching an inductive load — a relay coil, a solenoid — faces the same interruption hazard from Electrical Fundamentals: opening that current path abruptly induces a large voltage spike (\(V=L\,dI/dt\)) that can exceed the transistor's rated breakdown voltage and destroy it instantly. A flyback diode across the coil gives that stored energy a safe path to decay through, exactly as it does for any mechanical contact switching an inductive load — the transistor needs the identical protection a relay's own contacts would.

§4 · Solid-state relays versus electromechanical ones

A solid-state relay (SSR) uses a transistor (or triac, for AC) as the switching element, optically isolated from the control side — no moving parts, extremely fast switching, no contact bounce or wear, essentially unlimited switching life for a given current rating. An electromechanical relay (EMR) uses a physical armature and contacts — genuinely isolated by an air gap, tolerant of large inrush and surge currents an SSR of similar rating often cannot survive, and visibly, audibly confirmable as open or closed. An SSR wins where switching is frequent, fast, or silent operation matters (many cycles per minute, PWM-style control); an EMR wins where isolation certainty, surge tolerance, or simple visual/audible confirmation of state matters more than switching speed.

Key rule — switching, not amplifying

A switching transistor sits at cutoff (off) or saturation (on) — the linear region between them is crossed quickly, since that is where real power dissipation (and heat) occurs.

What this lesson assumes

  • Inductive-circuit interruption and flyback (Electrical Fundamentals, Lesson 3).

TermMeaningUse

quiz.q:
Where does the heat come from?

Problem. A switching transistor carries 2 A at a 0.2 V saturation drop when on, and blocks 24 V with negligible leakage current when off. Estimate the power dissipated in each state, and explain why a transistor lingering halfway between them would dissipate far more.
quiz.solution: Solution. Saturation: \(P=2\ \text{A}\times0.2\ \text{V}=0.4\ \text{W}\). Cutoff: current is negligible, so \(P\approx0\ \text{W}\) regardless of the 24 V blocked. Halfway between, both current and voltage could be substantial simultaneously — for example 1 A at 12 V gives 12 W, thirty times the saturation dissipation — which is exactly why a switching circuit is designed to cross that region quickly rather than idle there.

quiz.q:
Why the flyback diode is not optional

Problem. A transistor switches a relay coil, \(L=0.3\ \text{H}\), carrying 0.5 A, and turns off in about 2 microseconds with no flyback diode fitted. Estimate the induced voltage spike, and explain the consequence.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( V\approx L\,dI/dt=0.3\times(0.5/2\times10^{-6})=75{,}000\ \text{V} \) (an extreme, idealized estimate assuming the current is forced to zero that fast) — in practice the transistor breaks down and conducts destructively well before reaching that number, since its rated breakdown voltage is typically well under 100 V. Without a flyback diode, the coil’s stored energy has nowhere to go except through the transistor itself, destroying it.

quiz.q:
SSR or EMR?

Problem. A control system must switch a heater load a few times per hour, with occasional large inrush current at turn-on, and the maintenance team wants to be able to visually confirm the switch state from across the panel. Would a solid-state relay or an electromechanical relay be the better fit, and why?
quiz.solution: Solution. An electromechanical relay fits better: infrequent switching means SSR speed is not needed, the inrush tolerance favors mechanical contacts, and a visible/audible armature gives the maintenance team the confirmation an SSR (with no moving parts) cannot provide directly.

quiz.q: As a switch, a transistor is best operated:
choices: At cutoff or saturation, avoiding the linear region
Always in the linear region
Only ever at saturation, never cutoff
At a fixed midpoint bias
quiz.solution: The two extremes minimize power dissipation.

quiz.q: In cutoff, a transistor:
choices: Carries negligible current, regardless of blocked voltage
Carries maximum current
Has zero voltage across it
Dissipates maximum power
quiz.solution: Near-zero current means near-zero power, whatever the blocked voltage.

quiz.q: In saturation, a transistor:
choices: Has a small voltage drop, regardless of current level
Blocks all current
Dissipates the most power of any state
Is in its most fragile condition
quiz.solution: Small voltage drop means small power, whatever the current level.

quiz.q: The linear region between cutoff and saturation is avoided in switching applications because:
choices: Both current and voltage can be substantial there, producing significant power dissipation
It is electrically impossible to reach
It only exists in relays, not transistors
It has no effect on power dissipation
quiz.solution: Real power = current × voltage, and both factors can be large there simultaneously.

quiz.q: A flyback diode across a relay coil exists to:
choices: Give the coil's stored magnetic energy a safe path to decay through when switched off
Increase the coil's inductance
Prevent the coil from ever energizing
Reduce the supply voltage
quiz.solution: Without it, interrupting the coil current forces a destructive voltage spike.

quiz.q: A solid-state relay (SSR) compared to an electromechanical relay (EMR):
choices: Has no moving parts and switches much faster, with no contact wear
Always tolerates higher inrush current
Provides a visible confirmation of switch state
Is always the correct choice regardless of application
quiz.solution: Speed and switching life are the SSR's core advantages.

quiz.q: An electromechanical relay (EMR) is often preferred over an SSR when:
choices: Surge/inrush tolerance or visual confirmation of state matters more than switching speed
Extremely fast, frequent switching is required
Silent operation is the top priority
Contact wear is not a concern at all
quiz.solution: Mechanical contacts and an air gap offer isolation and surge tolerance an SSR often cannot match.

quiz.q: Power dissipated in a switching transistor is approximately:
choices: Collector current times the voltage across the transistor
Collector current alone
Voltage across the transistor alone
Independent of both current and voltage
quiz.solution: P = I × V is why cutoff and saturation both minimize dissipation, each for a different reason.

2.kuwait:
A control-panel output card running warm to the touch, with individual output transistors noticeably hotter than their neighbors switching identical loads, is a §2 diagnosis waiting to happen: a transistor stuck lingering in its linear region — from a marginal drive signal, a failing base resistor, or a load drawing more current than the output was rated for — dissipates real power as heat exactly where a healthy switching output would not, well before it fails outright.

A plant standardizing on solid-state relays for high-cycle-count applications (frequent starts/stops on a filling line, for instance) while keeping electromechanical relays for infrequent, high-inrush loads like large motor contactors is applying §4's trade-off deliberately, not by habit — mixing the two relay types intentionally across a panel, rather than defaulting to one type everywhere, is a sign of a design that actually thought about the application.

3.lecture:
§1 · Amplification, filtering, and instrumentation amplifiers

A basic op-amp amplifies the voltage difference between its two inputs by a large, controllable gain. Filtering — allowing some frequencies through while attenuating others — shapes a signal before or after amplification, removing noise components outside the frequency range the actual measurement occupies. An instrumentation amplifier is a specialized differential amplifier, built from op-amps, optimized specifically for small, noisy sensor signals: very high input impedance (so it does not load or disturb the sensor), precisely matched internal gain paths, and — critically — very high rejection of any signal that appears identically on both inputs at once.

§2 · Common-mode rejection as noise defense

Real field wiring runs through electrically noisy environments — motor drives, switching power supplies, nearby power cabling — and that noise tends to couple onto both wires of a signal pair roughly equally, since they run side by side. A signal appearing identically on both inputs is common-mode; the actual measurement is the small differential voltage between the two wires. Common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR), typically well over 80–100 dB for a good instrumentation amplifier, measures how strongly the amplifier suppresses the common-mode component while passing the differential signal through at full gain — CMRR does not reject noise in general; it specifically rejects whatever portion of the noise is identical on both input lines.

§3 · Why thermocouple microvolts survive a noisy plant at all

A thermocouple generates only tens of microvolts per degree of temperature difference — a signal easily smaller than the noise picked up by even a modest length of field wiring running near power cabling. What makes the measurement possible at all is that the noise, coupling onto both thermocouple leads roughly equally, arrives at the instrumentation amplifier as a large common-mode signal riding on top of the genuinely tiny differential signal — and a high-CMRR amplifier strips that common-mode part away, leaving the real microvolt-level signal largely intact. Twisted-pair wiring reinforces this by keeping both conductors physically close together, so they pick up nearly identical noise to begin with — a physical partner to the amplifier's own electronic rejection.

§4 · Working method

(1) Identify whether a signal is inherently small and differential — a thermocouple, an RTD bridge, a strain gauge — which flags it as needing an instrumentation amplifier rather than a simple single-ended op-amp stage. (2) Route it as a twisted, and ideally shielded, pair to keep common-mode pickup on both conductors as equal as possible. (3) Check the amplifier's CMRR spec against the expected noise environment — a higher CMRR is genuinely needed near variable-frequency drives and switching supplies than in a quiet instrument room. (4) Remember that CMRR only helps with noise common to both lines; differential noise — picked up unequally on the two conductors — passes through at full gain along with the real signal, which is exactly why twisted-pair routing matters as much as the amplifier's own spec.

Key idea — what CMRR actually rejects

CMRR rejects only the noise component identical on both input lines (common-mode) — differential noise, picked up unequally, passes through untouched.

  • Basic op-amp behavior (amplification of a voltage difference).
  • Thermocouples generate a small millivolt/microvolt-level signal (introduced here, developed fully in Lesson 6).

Toolkit — signal conditioning

TermMeaningUse
Instrumentation amplifierhigh-impedance differential ampStandard for small sensor signals.

quiz.q:
Why an instrumentation amplifier is needed

Problem. A thermocouple produces a differential signal of about 40 microvolts per °C, while noise coupled onto its wiring reaches several hundred millivolts common-mode. Explain why an ordinary single-ended amplifier would fail here, and what an instrumentation amplifier does differently.
quiz.solution: Solution. An ordinary single-ended amplifier has no way to distinguish the wanted differential signal from common-mode noise — it would amplify both together, burying the microvolt-level signal under a much larger noise floor. An instrumentation amplifier specifically rejects the common-mode component (via high CMRR) while amplifying only the small differential signal, making the true measurement recoverable.

quiz.q:
What CMRR does and does not fix

Problem. A thermocouple signal cable runs unshielded and untwisted right alongside a variable-frequency drive’s power cable, picking up noise unequally on its two conductors. Will a high-CMRR instrumentation amplifier fully solve this problem? Explain.
quiz.solution: Solution. No. CMRR only rejects noise that is identical (common-mode) on both input lines. Noise picked up unequally — because the cable is unshielded, untwisted, and routed asymmetrically near a strong noise source — behaves as differential noise, which passes through the amplifier at full gain along with the real signal. Proper twisted, shielded routing is needed alongside the amplifier's CMRR, not instead of it.

quiz.q:
Estimating rejected noise

Problem. An amplifier has CMRR = 100 dB. A common-mode noise voltage of 1 V appears at its input. Estimate the equivalent error this noise contributes at the input, referred through the CMRR figure.
quiz.solution: Solution. 100 dB corresponds to a rejection ratio of \(10^{100/20}=10^5\). The equivalent input-referred error is \(1\ \text{V}/10^5=10\ \mu\text{V}\) — small, but not zero, and worth comparing against the actual signal size (here, comparable to a thermocouple's own per-degree output) before assuming CMRR alone makes noise a non-issue.

quiz.q: An instrumentation amplifier is best described as:
choices: A specialized differential amplifier optimized for small, noisy sensor signals
A single-ended amplifier with no gain control
A device that only works with digital signals
A type of power supply regulator
quiz.solution: High input impedance and strong common-mode rejection define this class of amplifier.

quiz.q: A common-mode signal is:
choices: A signal appearing identically on both amplifier inputs
The actual measured differential signal
Always the wanted signal
Only present in digital circuits
quiz.solution: Common-mode is usually noise, not the measurement itself.

quiz.q: CMRR measures:
choices: How strongly an amplifier suppresses common-mode signal relative to differential signal
The amplifier's maximum output voltage
The amplifier's input impedance
The supply voltage required
quiz.solution: CMRR is a ratio, typically expressed in dB.

quiz.q: A thermocouple's differential signal is typically:
choices: Tens of microvolts per degree — far smaller than typical field noise
Several volts per degree
Identical in magnitude to typical field noise
Not affected by noise at all
quiz.solution: The signal's tiny size is exactly why noise rejection matters so much.

quiz.q: Twisted-pair wiring helps signal integrity mainly by:
choices: Keeping both conductors close together so they pick up nearly identical noise
Increasing the signal's own voltage
Eliminating the need for any amplifier
Only working for digital signals
quiz.solution: Equal noise pickup on both wires is what makes it truly common-mode, and therefore rejectable.

quiz.q: CMRR specifically rejects:
choices: Only the noise component identical on both input lines
All noise, regardless of how it was picked up
Only noise above 1 kHz
Only DC offsets
quiz.solution: Differential (unequally-coupled) noise is not addressed by CMRR at all.

quiz.q: Differential noise, picked up unequally on the two signal conductors:
choices: Passes through the amplifier at full gain, unaffected by CMRR
Is always fully rejected by CMRR
Cannot occur in properly wired systems
Only affects digital signals
quiz.solution: This is exactly why cable routing and shielding still matter alongside amplifier specs.

quiz.q: A higher CMRR is generally more important:
choices: Near strong noise sources such as variable-frequency drives
In a completely noise-free laboratory only
Only for digital logic signals
Only when no amplifier is used at all
quiz.solution: Higher common-mode noise levels demand stronger rejection to keep the true signal usable.

3.kuwait:
A thermocouple reading that jitters or drifts whenever a nearby variable-frequency drive starts is a classic §3/§4 symptom: the noise coupling onto the thermocouple's wiring may be well within the instrumentation amplifier's CMRR capability if it is genuinely common-mode, but a cable run untwisted or unshielded too close to the drive's power leads turns some of that noise differential — exactly the part CMRR cannot touch — and the practical fix is usually cable routing and shielding, not a better amplifier.

A field technician troubleshooting a "noisy" 4-20 mA or thermocouple signal checks the cable routing and grounding before ever suspecting the transmitter or amplifier itself, precisely because §4's working method puts wiring practice on equal footing with the electronics — a signal that looks fine on the bench can still be corrupted purely by how its cable was routed in the field.

4.lecture:
§1 · Logic levels, gates, and combinational thinking

Digital logic represents information as one of two levels — commonly \(0\ \text{V}\) (logic low) and a supply-referenced high (logic high, often \(24\ \text{V}\) in industrial I/O, or \(3.3/5\ \text{V}\) inside a processor). Basic gates (AND, OR, NOT, and combinations built from them) implement logical relationships between these levels — the foundation both of processor logic and of the simpler combinational thinking used in relay-replacement ladder logic.

§2 · Discrete inputs and outputs, as the PLC sees them

A discrete input reads one of two states — a switch open or closed, a proximity sensor detecting or not — and reports it to the controller as a logic level, not as an analog value; there is no "half-detected" reading. A discrete output similarly drives a field device — a solenoid, an indicator lamp, a small relay coil — to one of two states. This is the fundamental distinction from the analog signals covered elsewhere in this course: discrete I/O carries yes/no information, analog I/O carries a measured quantity across a continuous range.

§3 · Sinking versus sourcing — the wiring confusion resolved

The confusion is almost always about which side of the load a switching device controls, not about the load or supply themselves. A sourcing output supplies current — its output terminal sits at (or near) the positive supply voltage when active, with the load's other end returning to common (0 V). A sinking output instead provides a path to common — the load's other end connects to the fixed positive supply, and the output terminal itself pulls down to (near) 0 V when active. The load can be electrically identical in both cases; what differs is purely which terminal is switched and which is fixed. Mixing sourcing field devices with sinking-only PLC input cards (or the reverse) is a wiring mismatch that produces a device that appears to do nothing, or does the opposite of what is expected, even though every individual component tested fine in isolation.

§4 · Why PLCs read logic levels on discrete inputs

A discrete input card is built to distinguish only two states reliably, with margin against noise on either side, rather than to measure a precise voltage — it is designed for certainty (definitely on, definitely off), not precision. This is exactly why discrete and analog inputs are physically different card types on a PLC: an analog input card, by contrast, is built to resolve a continuous range accurately, at the cost of the simple robustness a discrete input's two-state design provides. Working method: (1) identify whether a signal is genuinely a yes/no state (discrete) or a measured quantity (analog); (2) for discrete I/O specifically, confirm sinking versus sourcing on both the field device and the PLC card before wiring, since this single detail — not the device's function — is the most common discrete-I/O wiring error.

Key distinction — sinking vs. sourcing

Sourcing: output supplies positive voltage, load returns to common. Sinking: output pulls to common, load's other end sits at fixed positive supply. Same load, opposite wiring.

quiz.q:
Diagnosing a sinking/sourcing mismatch

Problem. A sourcing proximity sensor is wired to a PLC input card that is also configured for sourcing inputs (rather than sinking). The sensor tests good on a bench meter, but the PLC never sees the input change state. Explain the likely cause.
quiz.solution: Solution. A sourcing sensor output must be paired with a sinking PLC input (one that pulls to common when active), not another sourcing device — two sourcing devices facing each other leaves no path to common for current to actually flow through the input circuit. The sensor itself can test perfectly fine in isolation while the pairing is simply wrong.

quiz.q:
Discrete vs. analog, correctly classified

Problem. Classify each as discrete or analog: (a) a level switch that trips at a fixed height, (b) a pressure transmitter reporting continuous pressure, (c) a motor-running indicator lamp.
quiz.solution: Solution. (a) Discrete — it reports only tripped or not tripped, no intermediate state. (b) Analog — it reports a continuous measured quantity across a range. (c) Discrete — the lamp is simply on or off, driven by a discrete output.

quiz.q:
Why not just use analog inputs for everything?

Problem. Explain why a PLC uses separate discrete and analog input card types, rather than reading every signal — switches included — through a single, more capable analog input card.
quiz.solution: Solution. A discrete input card is built for certainty against noise — it only needs to distinguish clearly on from clearly off, with margin either side, which makes it simple, robust, and cheap. An analog card is built for precision across a continuous range, which is unnecessary complexity and cost for a signal that only ever has two real states, and would not necessarily be more noise-robust for that simpler job.

quiz.q: A discrete input reports:
choices: One of two states, not a continuous measured value
A continuous analog value
Only AC signals
Only signals above 100 V
quiz.solution: There is no "half-detected" state for a discrete input.

quiz.q: A sourcing output:
choices: Supplies current toward the load, with the load's other end returning to common
Pulls the load's near end to common
Only works with AC loads
Cannot switch inductive loads
quiz.solution: The output terminal sits near supply voltage when active.

quiz.q: A sinking output:
choices: Pulls to common; the load's other end connects to a fixed positive supply
Supplies positive voltage directly to the load
Cannot be used with a PLC
Only works with discrete inputs, never outputs
quiz.solution: The switched terminal goes to near-0V when active, the opposite of sourcing.

quiz.q: Mixing a sourcing field device with a sourcing-only PLC input card:
choices: Produces a wiring mismatch, even though each device may test fine individually
Always works correctly regardless of type
Is the recommended standard practice
Only matters for analog signals
quiz.solution: Two sourcing devices facing each other leave no current path to common.

quiz.q: A discrete input card is designed primarily for:
choices: Certainty between two clear states, with noise margin
Precise measurement across a continuous range
AC signals exclusively
Eliminating the need for any wiring standard
quiz.solution: Robust on/off distinction, not fine resolution, is the design goal.

quiz.q: An analog input card is designed primarily for:
choices: Resolving a continuous range of values accurately
Only reading switch states
Higher noise immunity than a discrete card
Eliminating the need for calibration
quiz.solution: Precision across a range is the analog card's job, at some cost in simplicity.

quiz.q: The most common discrete-I/O wiring error is:
choices: Mismatching sinking and sourcing between the field device and the input/output card
Using the wrong wire gauge
Forgetting to power the PLC
Reversing an analog 4-20 mA loop
quiz.solution: This single detail causes far more "it doesn't work" calls than device failure does.

quiz.q: The key difference between discrete and analog I/O is:
choices: Discrete carries yes/no information; analog carries a measured quantity across a range
Discrete is always faster than analog
Analog only works with DC signals
There is no meaningful difference
quiz.solution: This distinction, not speed or voltage, is what separates the two I/O types.

4.kuwait:
A newly installed proximity sensor that "does not work" despite testing correctly on a bench meter is, in a large fraction of real service calls, a sinking-versus-sourcing mismatch rather than a faulty sensor — a technician who checks this specific pairing first, before condemning and replacing a perfectly good sensor, saves real time and a real parts cost on a routine call.

A plant standardizing all new discrete field devices on one convention — sourcing sensors paired with sinking PLC input cards, say — across every new installation, rather than leaving it to whichever convention a given vendor's catalog defaults to, is a small documentation decision that prevents exactly this class of wiring confusion from recurring project after project.

5.lecture:
§1 · The 4–20 mA loop, derived as an engineering decision

A current loop, rather than a voltage signal, is used for long field runs because current is unaffected by the voltage drop along the wire's own resistance — the same current flows at both ends regardless of cable length or gauge, unlike a voltage signal, which sags along a long run. The specific range \(4\)–\(20\ \text{mA}\), rather than \(0\)–\(20\ \text{mA}\), is a deliberate choice: \(4\ \text{mA}\) is the live zero — the minimum current the loop ever carries when everything is working, even at the very bottom of the measured range.

§2 · Why the live zero matters

Because a healthy loop never drops below \(4\ \text{mA}\), a reading of \(0\ \text{mA}\) is never a valid measurement — it can only mean a broken wire, a disconnected transmitter, or a dead power supply. This turns "signal absent" into an unambiguous, immediately recognizable fault condition rather than something that could be confused with a legitimate zero reading — a genuinely important safety and diagnostic property that a \(0\)–\(20\ \text{mA}\) (or a voltage) signal does not offer nearly as cleanly.

§3 · Reading the loop — the linear transfer function

The loop current maps linearly onto the measured range: \[ I=4+16\times\frac{x-x_{min}}{x_{max}-x_{min}}, \] where \(x\) is the measured quantity and \(x_{min}\), \(x_{max}\) define the transmitter's calibrated span. Reading the loop backward — from a measured current to the actual process value — inverts this directly: a transmitter spanning \(0\)–\(5\ \text{m}\) of level reading \(12\ \text{mA}\) corresponds to \((12-4)/16=0.5\), or exactly mid-scale, \(2.5\ \text{m}\).

§4 · A filling line's sensor set, walked end to end

A representative filling line uses several sensor families together: a level transmitter (often 4-20mA, as above) tracking tank level; a flow meter confirming actual delivered volume; temperature sensors protecting product quality or equipment; proximity or photoelectric sensors detecting container presence and position; pressure transmitters monitoring pump and line health. Each speaks its own native signal (4-20mA analog for continuous measurements, discrete on/off for presence detection, as in Lesson 4), and reading a control system's live data means recognizing which signal type each point actually is before interpreting the number on the screen — a "0" from a discrete proximity sensor means something entirely different from a "0 mA" fault on an analog loop, even though both might render as a red indicator on the same screen.

Key transfer function — 4-20 mA

\[ I = 4 + 16\left(\frac{x-x_{min}}{x_{max}-x_{min}}\right) \]

0 mA is always a fault — never a valid reading.

Reading a 4-20 mA loop

Problem. A transmitter spans 0-5 m of level and reads 12 mA. What is the level?

quiz.q:
Diagnosing a 0 mA reading

Problem. A 4-20 mA level transmitter suddenly reads 0 mA. Is this a valid (very low) level reading, or a fault? Explain, and name two possible causes.
quiz.solution: Solution. It is a fault, never a valid reading — the live zero guarantees at least 4 mA whenever the loop is working, even at the bottom of the measured range. Possible causes include a broken signal wire, a disconnected or failed transmitter, or a lost power supply to the loop.

quiz.q:
Converting the other direction

Problem. A pressure transmitter spans 0-10 bar. Find the loop current corresponding to a reading of 7.5 bar.
Current loops are used for long field wiring runs mainly because:
choices: Current is unaffected by voltage drop along the wire's own resistance
Current signals are always faster than voltage signals
Current loops require no power supply
Current loops cannot pick up noise
quiz.solution: The same current flows at both ends of the loop regardless of cable length.

quiz.q: The live zero in a 4-20 mA loop is:
choices: 4 mA, the minimum current a healthy loop ever carries
0 mA, the true zero of the range
20 mA, the top of the range
12 mA, the midpoint
quiz.solution: 4 mA represents the bottom of the measured range, not a broken loop.

quiz.q: A reading of 0 mA on a 4-20 mA loop indicates:
choices: A fault — a broken wire, dead transmitter, or lost power, never a valid measurement
A valid reading at the bottom of the range
Normal operation at half scale
The transmitter is over-range
quiz.solution: 0 mA is physically impossible during normal operation, which is exactly its diagnostic value.

quiz.q: The 4-20 mA transfer function is:
choices: Linear between the transmitter's calibrated minimum and maximum
Exponential
A step function
Undefined between 4 and 20 mA
quiz.solution: Current increases proportionally with the measured value across the span.

quiz.q: A voltage signal, compared to a current loop, over a long field wiring run:
choices: Sags due to voltage drop along the wire's resistance
Is always more accurate
Cannot be used at all over any distance
Never needs a live zero
quiz.solution: This is exactly the practical reason current loops dominate long analog field runs.

quiz.q: On a filling line, a discrete proximity sensor and an analog 4-20 mA transmitter:
choices: Use fundamentally different signal types that must be interpreted differently
Always use the identical signal convention
Can be freely substituted for one another
Both use a live zero at 4 mA
quiz.solution: Discrete carries yes/no state; analog carries a continuous measured value.

quiz.q: Reading a control system's live data correctly requires:
choices: Knowing which signal type (discrete or analog, and its span) each point actually represents
Assuming every value uses the same 4-20 mA convention
Ignoring the transmitter's calibrated span
Nothing beyond the raw displayed number
quiz.solution: The same displayed "0" can mean entirely different things depending on signal type.

quiz.q: A filling line's sensor set typically includes:
choices: Level, flow, temperature, presence-detection, and pressure sensors, each with its own signal type
Only a single universal sensor type for all measurements
Only discrete sensors, never analog
Only analog sensors, never discrete
quiz.solution: A real process line mixes multiple sensor families and signal types together.

5.kuwait:
A control-room alarm reading "0" on a tank-level loop is immediately actionable precisely because of §2's live-zero design: an experienced operator treats it as an instrument fault requiring dispatch, not as "the tank is empty," and dispatches a technician to check the loop wiring and transmitter power before ever assuming the process condition itself is the problem.

Commissioning a new filling-line sensor set means walking exactly §4's list, point by point — confirming each transmitter's actual calibrated span against the process drawing, not just that it powers up and reads something — a transmitter quietly ranged for the wrong span (0-10 m instead of the intended 0-5 m) will read a plausible-looking current at every level, just consistently wrong, until someone checks the range against the physical installation.

6.lecture:
§1 · RTD classes and self-heating

A resistance temperature detector (RTD) measures temperature by its own resistance change — the most common, a platinum Pt100, reads \(100\ \Omega\) at \(0^\circ\)C with a well-defined, highly linear resistance-versus-temperature relationship, giving RTDs excellent accuracy and repeatability compared to a thermocouple. But reading that resistance requires passing an excitation current through the RTD, and that current dissipates power, \(P=I^2R\), heating the element slightly above the true process temperature — self-heating. Because power scales with the square of current, a small increase in excitation current produces a disproportionately larger heating error: doubling excitation current quadruples self-heating, the identical relationship behind a loose electrical connection's dangerous heating. Self-heating is managed by keeping excitation current low and, where the process allows it, by good thermal contact between the RTD and a moving process fluid that carries the excess heat away.

§2 · Thermocouple types, cold junctions, and extension wire

A thermocouple generates a small voltage from the junction of two dissimilar metals, with the voltage depending on the temperature difference between the measuring junction and a reference — the cold junction. Because the thermocouple only ever reports a temperature difference, the instrument reading it must independently know the cold-junction temperature (measured separately, at the terminal block) and add it back in — cold-junction compensation — to recover the actual process temperature. Different thermocouple types (J, K, T, and others) use different metal pairs, each suited to a different temperature range and environment; extension wire — running from the cold junction back to the instrument — must be made of the same thermocouple alloy pair as the sensor itself, not ordinary copper wire, or a second, unaccounted-for junction is created partway along the run, corrupting the reading in a way that is easy to overlook and hard to diagnose from the symptom alone.

§3 · Thermowells and response lag

A thermowell is a closed-end tube inserted into a process, protecting the sensor inside it from direct contact with the process fluid — allowing sensor replacement without draining or depressurizing the process, and protecting against corrosion or erosion. That protection comes at a real cost: the thermowell's own thermal mass and the air gap (or fill fluid) between it and the sensor both slow the sensor's response to a genuine temperature change — response lag. A fast process upset can move faster than a thermowell-protected sensor can follow, a real limitation to weigh against the thermowell's real safety and maintainability benefits.

§4 · Choosing and installing a temperature point — the working method

(1) Match sensor type to the temperature range and required accuracy: RTDs for higher accuracy and stability at moderate temperatures, thermocouples for very high temperatures or where ruggedness matters more than ultimate precision. (2) Keep RTD excitation current low, and verify the specific installation's self-heating error against its actual accuracy requirement. (3) Use only matched extension wire for thermocouples, all the way to the cold-junction reference point. (4) Weigh a thermowell's protection and maintainability against its response-lag cost for the specific application — a slow-moving tank temperature can easily tolerate the lag; a fast-cycling process control loop may not.

Key relation — self-heating

\[ P = I^2R \Rightarrow \Delta T \propto I^2. \]

Same I²R relationship as a loose electrical connection — small current changes, large error changes.

What this lesson assumes

  • Power dissipation, P=I²R (Electrical Fundamentals, Lesson 1).
  • 4-20 mA signal basics (Lesson 5) — how a temperature transmitter reports its reading.

quiz.q:
Self-heating scales with current squared

Problem. An RTD self-heats by about 0.005 K at 1 mA excitation current. Estimate the self-heating error at 5 mA, and confirm the relationship with power.
quiz.solution: Solution. Since \( \Delta T\propto I^2 \), increasing current by a factor of 5 increases self-heating by \(5^2=25\): \(0.005\times25=0.125\ \text{K}\). This matches \(P=I^2R\) directly — a 5× current increase means 25× the dissipated power, and therefore 25× the temperature rise for the same thermal resistance.

quiz.q:
Diagnosing an extension-wire error

Problem. A type-K thermocouple is connected back to its instrument using ordinary copper extension wire instead of type-K extension wire. Explain what this does to the reading, and why the error is easy to overlook.
quiz.solution: Solution. The junction between the type-K thermocouple wire and the copper extension wire forms an unintended second thermocouple junction, at whatever temperature that connection happens to be — an error the instrument's cold-junction compensation, applied only at the true terminal block, does not account for. It is easy to overlook because the wiring may look correct at a glance, and the resulting error is often small enough to seem like ordinary measurement drift rather than an obvious fault.

quiz.q:
RTD or thermocouple — and why

Problem. A 90°C bearing housing needs a temperature measurement for a condition-monitoring program valuing accuracy and stability over the housing’s expected life. Which sensor type fits better, and on what grounds?
quiz.solution: Solution. An RTD fits better. At 90°C, well within an RTD's comfortable range, its superior accuracy, linearity, and long-term stability outweigh a thermocouple's advantages (wider range, ruggedness at very high temperature) that are not actually needed here — the RTD trades a modest self-heating concern, easily managed at low excitation current, for meaningfully better measurement quality at this specific, moderate temperature.

quiz.q: An RTD measures temperature by:
choices: Its own resistance change with temperature
Generating a voltage directly from temperature
Detecting infrared radiation
Measuring gas pressure
quiz.solution: Resistance, not voltage, is the RTD's native measured quantity.

quiz.q: RTD self-heating error:
choices: Grows with the square of excitation current
Is independent of excitation current
Decreases as excitation current increases
Only occurs at very low temperatures
quiz.solution: P=I²R means the error scales with current squared, not linearly.

quiz.q: A thermocouple's cold junction:
choices: Must be independently measured and compensated for to recover the true process temperature
Can always be ignored safely
Only matters for RTDs, not thermocouples
Is eliminated by using longer extension wire
quiz.solution: Cold-junction compensation is essential to thermocouple accuracy.

quiz.q: Thermocouple extension wire must be:
choices: The same alloy pair as the thermocouple itself
Ordinary copper wire, for lowest cost
Any wire rated for the voltage
Twisted but otherwise arbitrary material
quiz.solution: Mismatched wire creates an uncompensated second junction.

quiz.q: A thermowell primarily provides:
choices: Sensor protection and replaceability without draining or depressurizing the process
Faster sensor response
Elimination of self-heating error
Cold-junction compensation
quiz.solution: Protection and maintainability are the thermowell's core benefits.

quiz.q: The main cost of using a thermowell is:
choices: Slower response to genuine temperature changes (response lag)
Reduced measurement accuracy at steady state
Incompatibility with RTDs
Higher self-heating error
quiz.solution: Added thermal mass and an air/fluid gap both slow the response.

quiz.q: RTDs are generally preferred over thermocouples when:
choices: Higher accuracy and stability matter more than extreme temperature range or ruggedness
The absolute highest possible temperature must be measured
No excitation current is available
Cold-junction compensation is unavailable
quiz.solution: Thermocouples win on range and ruggedness; RTDs win on accuracy and stability.

quiz.q: Managing RTD self-heating mainly means:
choices: Keeping excitation current low
Increasing excitation current for a stronger signal
Removing the thermowell
Switching to a thermocouple in every case
quiz.solution: Lower current directly reduces the I²R heating error.

6.kuwait:
A maintenance shop stocking generic "thermocouple extension wire" without checking the type against each specific thermocouple in service is a quiet, ongoing source of small temperature-reading errors across a plant — §2's mismatched-junction problem does not announce itself loudly, it just makes affected loops read consistently a little off, in a way that is easy to blame on drift or calibration rather than trace to a wiring stock mix-up.

A bearing-temperature RTD reading that seems to run a fraction of a degree high compared to a calibrated reference, consistently across every unit on a given excitation-current setting, is a textbook §1 self-heating signature worth checking against the transmitter's excitation-current setting before assuming a calibration problem — sometimes simply lowering the excitation current, where the transmitter allows it, resolves the offset directly.

7.lecture:
§1 · Pressure: gauge, absolute, differential

Gauge pressure is measured relative to local atmospheric pressure — the everyday convention for most industrial readings. Absolute pressure is measured relative to a true vacuum, needed whenever atmospheric variation itself would corrupt the measurement (vacuum processes, some flow calculations). Differential pressure (DP) is the difference between two separate pressure points — neither necessarily atmospheric — and is the working principle behind both flow measurement (across a restriction) and, as this lesson develops, level measurement in a closed or pressurized tank.

§2 · Hydrostatic level measurement

A column of liquid of height \(h\) and density \(\rho\) exerts a pressure \(P=\rho g h\) at its base — the same hydrostatic relationship from Fluid Mechanics. An open tank, vented to atmosphere, needs only a single pressure tap near the bottom: the gauge pressure read there is directly proportional to level, since the atmosphere above the liquid contributes the same constant (zero, in gauge terms) regardless of level.

§3 · Closed tanks and the wet leg

A closed or pressurized tank complicates this directly: the vapor space above the liquid is no longer at atmospheric pressure, and that vapor pressure varies with process conditions, independent of level. A single bottom tap can no longer distinguish "vapor pressure changed" from "level changed." The fix is a genuine differential pressure measurement: the high side connects near the tank bottom (pressure \(=P_{vapor}+\rho g H_L\)), and the low side connects to the vapor space above the liquid — but doing so with a long impulse line means that line itself may fill with condensing process vapor, an uncontrolled and unpredictable column. The standard fix is a wet leg: the low-side impulse line is deliberately, permanently filled with a known reference fluid, so its contribution is fixed and calculable — \(P_{low}=P_{vapor}+\rho_{ref} g H_{wetleg}\) — rather than left to chance.

§4 · Zero suppression, and specifying the transmitter

Subtracting low from high, the vapor pressure term cancels exactly, leaving \(DP=\rho g H_L-\rho_{ref}gH_{wetleg}\) — a constant negative offset from the wet leg riding on top of the genuinely wanted level signal. Left uncorrected, the transmitter would read a large constant error at every level. The transmitter's calibration is instead zero-suppressed: its internal zero point is shifted to account for exactly the known, fixed wet-leg contribution, so the displayed output once again tracks only \(H_L\). Specifying a DP level transmitter for a closed tank therefore means fixing four things together: the process's actual level span, the reference fluid and wet-leg height (fixed by the physical installation), the resulting suppression value, and confirming the transmitter's own range can accommodate that shift without saturating.

Key relation — wet-leg suppression

\[ DP = \rho g H_L - \rho_{ref}\,g\,H_{wetleg} \]

The second term is fixed by installation — it is suppressed out in calibration, not measured.

What this lesson assumes

  • Hydrostatic pressure, \( P=\rho gh \) (Fluid Mechanics / Thermodynamics).
  • 4-20 mA transfer function (Lesson 5) — how the transmitter reports its (suppressed) reading.

quiz.q:
Computing the suppression value

Problem. A closed tank uses a water-filled wet leg, height 3 m, on the DP transmitter’s low side. Compute the fixed pressure offset this contributes, and state what must be done with it during calibration.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( P=\rho g H=1000\times9.81\times3=29{,}430\ \text{Pa}=29.43\ \text{kPa} \). This fixed value must be zero-suppressed — the transmitter's zero calibration shifted to remove it — so the displayed reading tracks only the actual tank level, not this constant wet-leg contribution.

quiz.q:
Open tank vs. closed tank measurement

Problem. Explain why an open tank can be leveled with a single pressure tap, while a closed, pressurized tank cannot.
quiz.solution: Solution. In an open tank, the space above the liquid is at atmospheric pressure — a constant that contributes nothing to the gauge-pressure reading, so a single bottom tap's reading is directly proportional to level. In a closed tank, the vapor space pressure varies with process conditions independent of level, so a single tap cannot distinguish a level change from a vapor-pressure change; a genuine differential measurement against the vapor space itself is required.

quiz.q:
Why not just vent the low side directly?

Problem. Explain why the low-side impulse line to a closed tank’s vapor space is deliberately filled with a reference fluid (a wet leg) rather than left as an open, gas-filled line.
quiz.solution: Solution. A gas-filled low-side line risks process vapor condensing inside it over time, creating an uncontrolled, unpredictable liquid column whose height (and therefore pressure contribution) cannot be known or corrected for. Deliberately filling the line with a known reference fluid to a fixed height makes that contribution fixed and calculable, so it can be reliably removed by zero suppression instead of drifting unpredictably.

quiz.q: Gauge pressure is measured relative to:
choices: Local atmospheric pressure
A true vacuum
The tank's vapor pressure
The wet leg's own pressure
quiz.solution: Absolute pressure, by contrast, is measured relative to a true vacuum.

quiz.q: An open tank can be leveled with a single pressure tap because:
choices: The space above the liquid is at constant atmospheric pressure
Open tanks never have vapor above the liquid
Gauge pressure is undefined for open tanks
A single tap always works regardless of tank type
quiz.solution: Atmospheric pressure contributes the same constant everywhere, canceling in the gauge reading.

quiz.q: A closed, pressurized tank requires differential pressure measurement for level because:
choices: Vapor space pressure varies independently of level, so a single tap cannot distinguish the two
Closed tanks cannot contain liquid
Differential pressure is easier to wire than gauge pressure
Closed tanks never need level measurement
quiz.solution: A single tap conflates level changes with vapor pressure changes in a closed tank.

quiz.q: A wet leg is:
choices: A low-side impulse line deliberately kept full of a known reference fluid
A high-side line filled with process liquid only
A type of pressure transmitter
A safety relief device
quiz.solution: Keeping it full makes its pressure contribution fixed and calculable.

quiz.q: Zero suppression in this context means:
choices: Shifting the transmitter's calibrated zero to remove a known, fixed offset
Setting the transmitter's output permanently to zero
Disabling the low-side pressure connection
Increasing the transmitter's span
quiz.solution: The wet leg's own constant weight is corrected out this way.

quiz.q: If the wet-leg suppression value is not correctly applied, the transmitter would:
choices: Read a constant offset error at every actual level
Read perfectly correctly regardless
Stop transmitting any signal
Only be wrong at the very top of the range
quiz.solution: The fixed offset appears at every level equally until it is corrected out.

quiz.q: Differential pressure (DP) measurement is also the working principle behind:
choices: Flow measurement across a restriction
Temperature measurement
Discrete proximity sensing
Digital logic level detection
quiz.solution: DP across an orifice or restriction is a standard flow-measurement technique.

quiz.q: Specifying a DP level transmitter for a closed tank requires knowing:
choices: The process level span, the wet-leg reference fluid and height, and the resulting suppression
Only the tank's overall volume
Only the transmitter's output signal type
Nothing beyond the desired 4-20 mA range
quiz.solution: All of these together determine the correct calibration.

7.kuwait:
A closed-tank level reading that is consistently, stubbornly wrong by what looks like a fixed offset — too high or too low by roughly the same amount no matter the actual level — is a §3/§4 symptom worth checking against the transmitter's suppression setting before assuming a sensor fault: a wet leg that was topped up, drained, or replaced with a different reference fluid during maintenance changes the required suppression value, and if the transmitter's calibration was not updated to match, the resulting error looks exactly like this.

A commissioning team documenting a new closed-tank installation records the wet-leg reference fluid and its exact height as part of the transmitter's calibration paperwork, not just the desired process range — because, per §4, the suppression value is fixed by the physical installation itself, and losing that documentation means a future recalibration has to re-derive it from scratch rather than simply reading it off the record.

8.lecture:
§1 · Why vibration is measured

Rotating machinery signals developing problems — bearing wear, imbalance, misalignment, looseness — well before those problems cause an outright failure, and each fault type tends to produce vibration at characteristic frequencies tied to shaft speed and component geometry. Condition-based monitoring tracks vibration levels over time specifically to catch this early signature, scheduling maintenance before a bearing actually seizes rather than running to failure or replacing on a fixed calendar regardless of actual condition.

§2 · The accelerometer

A piezoelectric accelerometer generates a small electrical charge proportional to the acceleration applied to it, exploiting the same piezoelectric effect used elsewhere in sensing — a crystal inside the sensor deforms slightly under acceleration, generating a proportional charge that conditioning electronics convert to a usable voltage signal. Because vibration is fundamentally an oscillating acceleration, this makes the accelerometer the standard sensor for machinery vibration monitoring.

§3 · Mounting method sets the usable range

An accelerometer does not measure the machine's vibration directly — it measures the vibration actually transmitted through its own mounting into the sensor. Every mounting method has its own mechanical resonance, above which the mounting itself distorts the reading rather than faithfully transmitting it. In descending order of usable frequency range: a stud mount (threaded directly into a drilled, tapped hole) gives the most rigid coupling and the highest usable frequency range; an adhesive pad mount is nearly as good without requiring drilling; a magnetic mount is fast and fully non-invasive but its usable range drops noticeably, since the magnetic coupling itself is less rigid; a handheld probe (pressed against the machine by hand) is the least rigid of all, usable only for low-frequency, quick spot checks. A high-frequency bearing defect frequency, read through a magnetic mount whose flat range has already rolled off, can be significantly under-read or missed entirely — not because the sensor is faulty, but because the mounting itself cannot faithfully transmit that frequency.

§4 · Speed and proximity sensors

Vibration analysis is far more diagnostic when correlated with actual shaft speed, since most machinery fault frequencies scale directly with RPM. A tachometer or a proximity probe aimed at a shaft keyway or a once-per-revolution reflective mark provides a speed reference signal, letting vibration frequencies be expressed as orders of running speed (1×, 2×, 3× RPM, and so on) rather than raw Hz — a much more directly diagnostic way to distinguish imbalance (typically 1× RPM) from misalignment (typically 2× RPM) from other fault signatures.

Key ranking — mounting rigidity

Stud > adhesive > magnet > handheld probe, in both usable frequency range and measurement repeatability.

  • Basic familiarity with rotating machinery (imbalance, misalignment) from Kinematics of Machinery, if taken.

quiz.q:
Choosing a mounting method

Problem. A bearing defect of interest occurs at a frequency of about 6 kHz. Which mounting method(s) from §3 would reliably capture it, and which would not? Explain.
quiz.solution: Solution. A stud mount (flat to roughly 8 kHz) would reliably capture a 6 kHz signal. An adhesive mount (flat only to roughly 4 kHz) would already be rolling off at 6 kHz and would under-read it. A magnetic mount (flat only to roughly 2 kHz) would badly under-read or miss it, and a handheld probe would be worse still — none of the less rigid mounts can faithfully transmit a frequency that high.

quiz.q:
Diagnosing an apparently healthy bearing

Problem. A magnetically mounted accelerometer shows a healthy-looking vibration spectrum on a bearing later found, on teardown, to have a well-developed high-frequency defect. Explain how the mounting choice could be responsible for missing it.
quiz.solution: Solution. If the defect frequency lies above the magnetic mount's usable flat range, the mount's own resonance and roll-off suppress the signal before it ever reaches the sensor element faithfully — the spectrum can look clean not because the machine is healthy, but because the mounting itself filtered out the evidence. A more rigid mount (stud or adhesive) extending flat response further would be needed to actually see that defect frequency.

quiz.q:
Interpreting vibration as orders of speed

Problem. A machine runs at 1800 RPM (30 Hz). A vibration analyzer shows a strong peak at 60 Hz. Expressed as an order of running speed, what is this, and what fault does it typically suggest?
quiz.solution: Solution. \(60/30=2\), so this is the 2× running-speed order — typically associated with misalignment, distinct from the 1× order (30 Hz) that would typically suggest imbalance instead.

quiz.q: Condition-based monitoring of rotating machinery aims to:
choices: Catch developing faults early, before they cause outright failure
Replace components on a fixed calendar schedule regardless of condition
Eliminate the need for any maintenance
Only detect faults after failure has occurred
quiz.solution: This is the core value proposition over both run-to-failure and fixed-interval maintenance.

quiz.q: A piezoelectric accelerometer generates:
choices: A small electrical charge proportional to applied acceleration
A resistance change proportional to temperature
A current proportional to light intensity
A frequency proportional to pressure
quiz.solution: This is the piezoelectric effect applied to vibration sensing.

quiz.q: In order of usable frequency range, from highest to lowest:
choices: Stud, adhesive, magnet, handheld probe
Magnet, adhesive, stud, handheld probe
Handheld probe, magnet, adhesive, stud
All mounting methods give identical frequency range
quiz.solution: Mounting rigidity directly determines the usable frequency range.

quiz.q: A high-frequency signal read through a mount whose usable range has already rolled off will be:
choices: Under-read or missed, even though the sensor itself is functioning correctly
Read with perfect accuracy regardless of mount
Amplified beyond its true value
Converted automatically to a lower, correct frequency
quiz.solution: The mounting's own resonance and roll-off distort the measurement above its usable range.

quiz.q: A magnetic mount's main advantages are:
choices: Speed and being fully non-invasive, with no drilling required
The highest possible frequency range of any mounting method
Eliminating the need for any accelerometer
Guaranteed accuracy at all frequencies
quiz.solution: Convenience trades off against reduced usable frequency range compared to a stud mount.

quiz.q: A tachometer or proximity probe aimed at a shaft is used to:
choices: Provide a speed reference so vibration can be expressed as orders of running speed
Directly measure vibration amplitude
Replace the need for an accelerometer entirely
Measure temperature at the bearing
quiz.solution: Expressing frequencies as orders of RPM is far more directly diagnostic than raw Hz alone.

quiz.q: A vibration peak at 1× running speed typically suggests:
choices: Imbalance
Misalignment
A bearing defect
Electrical noise only
quiz.solution: Different fault types have characteristic, though not universal, order signatures.

quiz.q: A handheld probe, compared to stud, adhesive, and magnetic mounts:
choices: Has the lowest usable frequency range and repeatability of the four
Has the highest usable frequency range
Is the standard for precision condition monitoring
Requires no accelerometer at all
quiz.solution: It is reserved for quick, low-frequency spot checks, not precision trending.

8.kuwait:
A condition-monitoring program that standardizes on stud-mounted accelerometers at permanent, documented locations on critical rotating equipment — rather than a technician touching a magnetic probe to whatever surface is convenient each round — is applying §3's ranking deliberately: the extra installation effort buys both a wider trustworthy frequency range and, just as importantly, a repeatable measurement location that makes trend comparisons over months meaningful rather than confounded by mounting variation.

A vibration analyst reviewing a spectrum first checks what mounting method produced it, and what running speed the machine was at, before reading anything into a peak at a specific frequency — per §3 and §4, both the trustworthy frequency range and the diagnostic meaning of a given frequency depend on that context, not on the raw number alone.

9.lecture:
§1 · Analog-to-digital conversion

An ADC converts a continuous analog voltage into a discrete digital number, at a moment in time it is triggered to sample. Two independent specifications govern how faithfully that conversion represents the original signal: resolution — how many discrete levels the ADC can distinguish, set by its bit depth (a 12-bit ADC resolves \(2^{12}=4096\) levels across its input range) — and sampling rate — how often, in samples per second (Hz), the ADC takes a new reading. Resolution limits how finely a single reading is known; sampling rate limits how fast a changing signal can be tracked at all.

§2 · The Nyquist criterion

The Nyquist criterion states that a signal must be sampled at a rate at least twice its highest frequency component to be reconstructed unambiguously — \(F_s>2f_{max}\). Half the sampling rate, \(F_s/2\), is the Nyquist frequency: the highest frequency that can be faithfully captured at that sampling rate. A signal containing energy above the Nyquist frequency is not simply lost — it reappears, corrupted, as described next.

§3 · Aliasing — a false frequency, not a missing one

When a signal above the Nyquist frequency is sampled anyway, it does not vanish — it aliases, reappearing in the digitized data as a false signal at a lower frequency, indistinguishable from a genuine signal at that lower frequency. A signal at \(700\ \text{Hz}\), sampled at \(F_s=1000\ \text{Hz}\) (Nyquist frequency \(500\ \text{Hz}\)), aliases to \(|700-1000|=300\ \text{Hz}\) — the digitized data looks exactly as if a real \(300\ \text{Hz}\) signal had been sampled, and no processing performed after the fact on the digital samples alone can tell the two apart, because the samples themselves are identical either way.

§4 · Anti-aliasing filters — fixing it before, not after

Because aliasing is unrecoverable after digitization — the information distinguishing "real 300 Hz" from "aliased 700 Hz" is simply gone from the samples — the only correct fix is prevention: an anti-aliasing filter, an analog low-pass filter placed before the ADC, removing any signal content above the Nyquist frequency before sampling ever occurs. Choosing a sampling rate for a new measurement therefore means identifying the highest genuine frequency of interest first (a bearing defect frequency, a fast pressure transient), setting \(F_s\) comfortably above twice that value, and fitting an anti-aliasing filter tuned to the resulting Nyquist frequency — sampling rate and filter are chosen together, not independently.

Key relation — Nyquist and aliasing

\[ F_s > 2f_{max}, \qquad f_{alias}=|f_{true}-F_s| \ \text{(when } f_{true}>F_s/2 \text{)} \]

quiz.q:
Computing an alias frequency

Problem. A 700 Hz vibration signal is sampled at 1000 Hz, with no anti-aliasing filter fitted. What frequency will it falsely appear as in the digitized data?
quiz.solution: Solution. Nyquist frequency \(=1000/2=500\ \text{Hz}\); since 700 Hz exceeds this, it aliases to \(|700-1000|=300\ \text{Hz}\) — the digitized data is indistinguishable from a genuine 300 Hz signal.

quiz.q:
Choosing a sampling rate

Problem. A bearing defect frequency of interest is expected up to 4 kHz. Propose a minimum sampling rate satisfying the Nyquist criterion, and explain why choosing exactly \(2\times4\ \text{kHz}=8\ \text{kHz}\) would be a poor practical choice even though it technically satisfies the criterion.
quiz.solution: Solution. The Nyquist criterion requires \(F_s>2f_{max}\), strictly greater than, not equal to — sampling at exactly 8 kHz leaves no margin, and any real anti-aliasing filter has a gradual roll-off rather than a perfectly sharp cutoff, so some content just above 4 kHz could still leak through and alias. A practical choice would sample comfortably above 8 kHz (commonly 2.5–5× the frequency of interest) to give the anti-aliasing filter real margin to work with.

quiz.q:
Why filtering after sampling cannot fix aliasing

Problem. Explain why a digital low-pass filter applied to already-sampled data cannot undo aliasing that occurred during sampling.
quiz.solution: Solution. Once sampled, a false 300 Hz alias and a genuine 300 Hz signal produce numerically identical sample sequences — the information that would distinguish them was never captured in the first place. A digital filter can only operate on the samples it is given, and has no way to recover a distinction that does not exist in that data; the only correct fix is an analog anti-aliasing filter applied before sampling, removing the offending frequency content before it can alias.

quiz.q: ADC resolution refers to:
choices: The number of discrete levels the ADC can distinguish, set by bit depth
How often the ADC samples per second
The maximum voltage the ADC can measure
The ADC's power consumption
quiz.solution: Sampling rate is a separate specification, governing how often, not how finely.

quiz.q: The Nyquist criterion states that sampling rate must be:
choices: Greater than twice the highest frequency component in the signal
Exactly equal to the highest frequency component
Less than half the highest frequency component
Irrelevant to signal reconstruction
quiz.solution: \( F_s>2f_{max} \) is required for unambiguous reconstruction.

quiz.q: The Nyquist frequency is:
choices: Half the sampling rate
Equal to the sampling rate
Always exactly 1 kHz
Independent of sampling rate
quiz.solution: \( F_s/2 \) is the highest frequency capturable without aliasing.

quiz.q: Aliasing occurs when:
choices: A signal above the Nyquist frequency is sampled and reappears as a false lower frequency
The ADC's resolution is too low
The signal amplitude exceeds the ADC's input range
The sampling rate is too high
quiz.solution: Undersampling, not over-range amplitude or low resolution, causes aliasing.

quiz.q: Once a signal has aliased into digitized samples:
choices: No digital processing on those samples alone can recover the true frequency
A digital low-pass filter always corrects it after the fact
It can be identified and removed by resolution alone
It has no effect on the data at all
quiz.solution: The distinguishing information was never captured — it cannot be recovered afterward.

quiz.q: An anti-aliasing filter is:
choices: An analog low-pass filter placed before the ADC
A digital filter applied after sampling
A type of accelerometer mount
Only needed for discrete signals
quiz.solution: Prevention before sampling is the only real fix for aliasing.

quiz.q: Choosing a sampling rate for a new measurement should be based on:
choices: The highest genuine frequency of interest, with margin above twice that value
The ADC's maximum possible rate, always
The lowest frequency of interest only
An arbitrary round number with no calculation
quiz.solution: The Nyquist criterion, with practical margin, drives this choice directly.

quiz.q: Sampling rate and anti-aliasing filter selection should be:
choices: Chosen together, matched to the same frequency of interest
Chosen completely independently of one another
Irrelevant to each other
Only the filter matters; sampling rate is arbitrary
quiz.solution: The filter must remove content above the Nyquist frequency that the chosen sampling rate implies.

9.kuwait:
A vibration spectrum showing an unexpected, physically implausible peak at a frequency that does not correspond to any known machine component or fault is worth checking against §3 before treating it as real: an inadequately filtered high-frequency vibration component aliasing down into the analyzed range can produce a peak that looks meaningful but is actually a sampling artifact, not a genuine mechanical signature.

A data acquisition system being specified for a new high-speed process measurement documents both the sampling rate and the anti-aliasing filter's cutoff together, as a matched pair, in the same specification sheet — per §4, quoting one without the other leaves the measurement's actual frequency validity unstated, a gap a careful commissioning review should catch before the system is accepted.

10.lecture:
§1 · Ground is not one number everywhere

Ground symbols on a schematic suggest a single, shared \(0\ \text{V}\) reference — in practice, a real plant's grounding grid has resistance, and different equipment grounded at physically distant points can sit at slightly different actual potentials, especially when other equipment (large motors, welding, switching drives) injects current into that same grid. This ground potential difference, often just a volt or two, is normally harmless — until a cable's shield connects two such points directly together.

§2 · How a ground loop forms

A cable shield grounded at both its field end and its control-room end forms a closed electrical path between those two ground points. If those two points sit at even a small potential difference, that difference drives a real current through the shield — a ground loop current — entirely independent of, and in addition to, the shield's intended job of blocking external electromagnetic interference. That circulating current can couple into the actual signal conductors, appearing as noise, drift, or an offset error that has nothing to do with the process being measured.

§3 · The fix — single-point grounding

Grounding the shield at only one end — conventionally the source or control-room end, with the far end left floating (not connected to local ground) — leaves the loop open. No closed path exists, so no current can flow through the shield regardless of how different the two ground potentials are, while the shield still performs its intended job of intercepting external interference and carrying it away at the single grounded end. This is why a shielded cable correctly landed at one end, and correctly left open at the other, is not a wiring shortcut — it is the deliberate fix for a real, measurable failure mode.

§4 · When isolation is worth the added cost

Sometimes both ends genuinely need a local ground connection for their own independent safety or EMI reasons, or the expected potential difference is too large to leave to single-point grounding alone. A signal isolator (or isolation amplifier) uses a transformer or optical coupling to pass the signal's information across a gap with no direct electrical (galvanic) connection between the two sides — each side can be grounded locally as needed, with no possible path for a loop current to form between them, at the cost of an added component in the signal path. Choosing between single-point grounding and full galvanic isolation is a judgment call based on the expected potential difference, the distance involved, and whether both ends have an independent reason to be locally grounded.

Key rule — shield grounding

Ground a cable shield at ONE end only. Grounding both ends turns any ground potential difference into a driving voltage for a loop current.
10.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Common-mode noise and twisted-pair wiring (Lesson 3) — the shield is a second, complementary layer of noise defense.
  • 4-20 mA current loops for long field runs (Lesson 5).
